coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dots from maple tree leaves for biosensing of Cesium and electrocatalytic oxidation of glycerol
coredata.dc:creator: Chellasamy, Gayathri
Arumugasamy, Shiva Kumar
Govindaraju, Saravanan
Yun, Kyusik
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from biological sources play a significant role in biomedical and environmental applications, including bioimaging, biosensing, metal ions detection and electrocatalytic oxidations. Herein, we synthesized blue-emitting carbon quantum dots using maple tree leaves via a one-step hydrothermal process to detect Cesium ions selectively. The synthesized CQDs' functional group composition, morphology, and pH stability was analytical and morphologically investigated. The maple leaves derived carbon quantum dots (M-CQDs) exhibited blue fluorescence, and their sizes ranged from 1 to 10 nm. They exhibited emission at 445 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. M-CQDs PL intensity was highly stable for about 100 d without any changes and confirmed that the as-prepared CQDs could be used as a probe for Cesium ion sensing. M-CQDs were effectively used as Cesium sensing probes based on the electron transfer process and simultaneously used as a catalyst for glycerol electrooxidation. The PL intensity of M-CQDs was quenched while adding the varies concentration of Cesium ions in the linear range from 100 μM to 100 nM with the detection limit of (LOD) 160 nM, simultaneously electrocatalytic oxidation of glycerol showed an onset potential of 1.32 V at a current density of 10 mA/cm2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Cesium
Maple leaves
Glycerol electrooxidation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of Cesium in real sample
Morphological and analytical characterization of MCQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation ( 2021R1A2C1011858 ). This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2021R1A6A1A03038996 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of MCQDs, Inset: Particle size distribution, (b) AFM image of MCQDs. (c) 3D image of MCQDs (d) Line graph of AFM image.

FT-IR spectrum of M-CQDs (red trace), Cesium (blue trace), M-CQDs + Cesium (black trace). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
a) Survey XPS spectra of MCQDs and MCQDs + Cesium, (b–d) Deconvoluted high resolution XPS spectra of MCQDs C 1s, N 1s, O 1s.
a) Raman spectrum of M-CQDs and M-CQDs + Cesium, b) XRD spectrum of MCQDs.

a) UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of MCQDs. Inset: images of the MCQDs aqueous solution in visible light (right) and ultraviolet radiation (left) b) Fluorescence emission spectra of the MCQDs excited at various wavelengths.
a) Fluorescence spectrum of MCQDs in the presence of a different concentration of Cesium in DI water, b) Linear relationship between F0/F and Cesium concentration in the range of 100 nM-100 μM.

a) Fluorescence spectrum of MCQDs in the presence of a different concentration of Cesium in seawater b) Corresponding Linear regression plot of MCQDs with Cesium in seawater, c) Fluorescence spectrum of MCQDs in the presence of a different concentration of Cesium in tap water, d) Corresponding Linear regression plot of MCQDs with Cesium in tap water.

a) Cyclic voltammogram analysis in the presence of M-CQDs with various solvents b) Linear Sweep voltammetry analysis in the presence of M-CQDs with various solvents; c) Nyquist analysis for M-CQDs in various solvents; d) Chronoamperometric study for M-CQDS @ 1.32 V in KOH + Gly.
The schematic representation of MCQDs synthesis and the applications.
A comparison of different detecting systems for Cesium determination.

head.ce:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dots from maple tree leaves for biosensing of Cesium and electrocatalytic oxidation of glycerol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Gyeonggi-do, 13120, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bionanotechnology, Gachon University, Gyeonggi-do, 13120, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized from biological sources play a significant role in biomedical and environmental applications, including bioimaging, biosensing, metal ions detection and electrocatalytic oxidations. Herein, we synthesized blue-emitting carbon quantum dots using maple tree leaves via a one-step hydrothermal process to detect Cesium ions selectively. The synthesized CQDs' functional group composition, morphology, and pH stability was analytical and morphologically investigated. The maple leaves derived carbon quantum dots (M-CQDs) exhibited blue fluorescence, and their sizes ranged from 1 to 10 nm. They exhibited emission at 445 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. M-CQDs PL intensity was highly stable for about 100 d without any changes and confirmed that the as-prepared CQDs could be used as a probe for Cesium ion sensing. M-CQDs were effectively used as Cesium sensing probes based on the electron transfer process and simultaneously used as a catalyst for glycerol electrooxidation. The PL intensity of M-CQDs was quenched while adding the varies concentration of Cesium ions in the linear range from 100 μM to 100 nM with the detection limit of (LOD) 160 nM, simultaneously electrocatalytic oxidation of glycerol showed an onset potential of 1.32 V at a current density of 10 mA/cm2.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots(CQDs) produced from natural source plays a significant role in biomedical applications, due to its enormous properties including the highly stable nature, long-lasting fluorescence property, biocompatibility, less toxicity, easy availability of the source. These outstanding properties of CQDs make them readily available for a wide range of applications like biosensing of organic molecules, detection of metal ions and a radioactive isotope, bioimaging, drug delivery and catalytic applications [Zhang et al., 2019a, 2019b]. In addition to CQDs wide range of applications, its synthesis is also done by distinct methods, including microwave assistance method, one-pot hydrothermal approach, and ultra-sonic methods. To make CQDs more effective towards the application, they were surface passivated or functionalized [Lim et al., 2015].

Cesium, a highly volatile alkali metal, though it possesses commercial applications in photoelectric cells and atomic clocks, is mildly toxic. Its radioactive nature makes it a hazardous pollutant to the environment, which in turn causes harmful health issues. Hence it is significant to detect the Cesium ions employing feasible sensing methods. Among the available sensing methods, fluorescence-based optical sensing has significant benefits like high sensitivity, low detection range, precise and easy handling, less laborious. We used green synthesized carbon quantum dots as a probe for fluorescence sensing of Cesium [Kumar et al., 2016]. Simultaneously the CQDs were effectively used for electrocatalytic oxidation of glycerol. Electrocatalytic oxidation of Glycerol majorly focused on simultaneous conversion into high-value goods, namely C3 acids (tartronic, glycolic, glyceric), oxalic acid, formic acid, dihydroxyacetone and glyceraldehyde [Katryniok et al., 2011]. At the same time, these products are utilized for fuel cell generation due to their enormous power density and partial fuel usage. The biomass-derived quantum dots are potential candidates extending meritorious performance for the oxidation process. Initially, electrocatalytic oxidation performed in optimal conditions, followed by eliminating the necessity of chemical oxidants exhausting the toxic pollutants from the environment. Coupling of oxidation and reduction reactions with the biomass-derived quantum dots aids in the simultaneous generation of hydrogen gas at the cathode and high-value goods at the anode, boosting the cost-consciousness of the electrocatalytic reactions. Incidentally, such an oxidation process provides another phase for generating high-value goods by converting Glycerol through catalytic oxidation in a selective approach [Fan et al., 2020; Han et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2021]. Over the past decades of catalytic studies for glycerol oxidation, mostly noble metals and their alloys are utilized generating complex intermediates, prominent to low selectivity.

We synthesized carbon quantum dots in this work using maple tree leaves as a carbon source via the one-step hydrothermal method. The as-synthesized CQDs exhibits strong blue fluorescence, which is highly stable, and thus it is effectively used for biosensing and electrocatalytic application. Thus, a systematic investigation of cost-effective biomass-derived quantum dots developed for radioactive element sensing through fluorescent technique; additionally, for generating high-value goods through electrocatalytic oxidation of Glycerol.

Maple leaves were collected from Gachon university global campus trees. Cesium bromide was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Deionized (DI) water purified by a Milli-Q Purification System (Millipore, USA) was used for all experiments.

An ATTO UV Transilluminator was utilized to acquire luminescent pictures at 365 nm of the MCQDs. A Varian Cary 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–vis absorption spectra. Photoluminescence (PL) results were recorded utilizing a SCINCO FS-2 Fluorescence Spectrometer, with FluoroMaster Plus programming, 150W Continuous Wave Xenon-Arc Lamp and superior R-928 PMT locator. HR-TEM pictures and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were recorded utilizing a Tecnai™ G2 F30 Series. The height profile of MCQDs was imaged by atomic force microscopy (AFM, JPK NanoWizard II Atomic force microscope, Berlin, Germany). JASCO FT/IR 4600 provided with spectral analysis software was used to perform Fourier transform infrared spectrograph. Raman spectral analysis was done utilizing a Micro-Raman system (Ramboss 500i) with a He-NE laser beam at 633 nm. Teflon autoclave was used for the synthesis of MCQDs.

A one-step hydrothermal process synthesized MCQDs. Briefly, maple leaves were collected from trees around the university campus. The collected leaves were washed with deionized water, and it is blended using a domestic blender. A 50 mL of as-obtained blended solution was poured in a Teflon Lined hydrothermal reactor. Followed by the reactor was placed in a laboratory oven at 190 °C for 8 h. After 8 h of reaction, the reactor was allowed to cool naturally at room temperature. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant is collected and filtered using filter paper and stored at 4 °C.

The synthesized MCQDs were used as a probe to sense Cesium via fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence measurements were taken as follows, in a cuvette, 100 μL of MCQDs, followed by an addition of 100 μL of prepared Cesium and then finally 2 mL of DI water was added and mixed well pipetting and allowed to incubate for 5 min to record measurements. The sensing process of Cesium takes about 10 min which includes incubation time and the response readout time. Throughout the study 1.5 mg/ml of MCQDs were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Cesium in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: For real sample analysis of this sensor, water was collected from the Incheon sea, South Korea. The collected seawater was filtered with filter paper to remove the impurities. Different Cesium ions (100 nM-100 μM) were prepared, and they were spiked into the seawater. The MCQDs were used as probes with the different concentrations of spiked seawater samples for recording the PL spectrum, and the sensing was done.

Interferents such as arsenic, lead, cadmium, nickel, mercury, zinc, chromium was used for the interferents study. The experiment is proceeded as follows where 100 μM interferent solutions were prepared. In cuvette 100 μL MCQDs were taken, to which the prepared interferent solution were added and 3 mL of DI were added to cuvette and allowed to incubate for 5 min. Then the PL spectra were recorded and analyzed.

The catalytic activities of the biomass-derived electrocatalysts, evaluated using cyclic voltammograms (CV) analysis conducted in a medium comprising of 0.5M H2SO4, 0.5 M KOH and 0.5 M glycerol at a potential range of −0.5 to 1 V (vs Ag/AgCl) using a scan rate of 0.05 V/s. Before the electrochemical studies, the catalytic solution amalgamated with 5% Nafion and sonicated for 30 min. About 20 μL of the catalytic composition is drop-casted on the well-polished GCE surface. All the electrochemical tests completed using the VersaSTAT3 instrument with ZView software for Nyquist plot analysis. The electroactive surface area (ECSA) defined using the Randles-Sevcik formula
Ip = 2.69 × 1
0 5
×
AD
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
γ
1 / 2
C
where n is the number of the electrons involved in the oxidation process, A is electrode surface area (cm2), γ is scan rate (V/s), D is the diffusion coefficient of the molecules in the electrolyte (cm2/s), and C is the concentration of the probe molecule in the electrolyte medium (mol/cm3).

Herein, we report a one pot hydrothermal synthesis of MCQDs using maple tree leaves as carbon source. The cleaned and blended leaves paste was hydrothermally treated in Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave at 190 °C for 8 h. After the reaction period it is allowed to attain the room temperature. Then the reaction mixture is centrifuged to remove the debris and the supernatant is collected. Collected supernatant is filtered by using filter paper to further remove the debris. Finally, the obtained solution emitted blue fluorescence under UV illumination, which confirms the formation of MCQDs. The synthesized MCQDs were optimized for pH and time for effective sensing and electrocatalytic application. The MCQDs shown the highest intensity at pH 7 (Fig. S1a) and displayed stable intensity in the basic conditions from 7 to 9 pH. In case of time the MCQDs shown stable intensity for about 120 days, which confirms that the as-synthesized MCQDs were highly stable (Fig. S1b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and analytical characterization of MCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Raman analysis of MCQDs shows distinctive peaks at 1428 cm−1 (D band) and 1671 cm−1 (G band), the intensity ratio of the G band to D band of MCQDs was calculated to be 0.854, which represents the as-synthesized MCQDs has graphitic crystalline nature (Fig. 4 a) [Dager et al., 2020]. Raman analysis of MCQDs with Cesium shows mild shifting in peaks with a decrease in intensity. XRD analysis of as-synthesized MCQDs shows a peak at 20° (Fig. 4b) which attributes the turbostratic carbon structure [Bajpai et al., 2019]. These analytical characteristics confirm that the as-synthesized MCQDs were beneficial towards the detection and electrooxidation applications.

To further investigate the optical characteristics of synthesized MCQDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded, which shows a peak at 290 nm attributing to n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of CC (Fig. 5 a), and the emission of M-CQDs was observed at 445 nm when excited at 360 nm. The aqueous solution of MCQDs was pale yellow colour under visible light, and under UV light, it emitted blue fluorescence. The excitation dependent emission is studied in the range of 300–400 nm, in which the highest emission is observed at the wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 5b) [Zhang et al., 2019a, 2019b].

The fluorescence sensing of Cesium using MCQDs is shown in (Fig. 6a). In addition to various Cesium concentrations from 100 nM to 100 μM, the fluorescence intensity of MCQDs was quenched drastically. The relative change in the fluorescence intensity versus the initial intensity (F0/F) at 360 nm excitation as a function of Cesium concentration in the linear range of 100 nM to 100 μM with a regression coefficient R2 = 0.99232 is presented in the inset of (Fig. 6b).

To investigate the relationship between the concentration of Cesium and the quenching efficiency of MCQDs utilized the Stern-Volmer equation
F 0
/ F = 1 + Ks
[ Q ]
F0 and F are the emission intensities at 450 nm in the absence and presence of Cesium; respectively, Ks stands for Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is the concentration of Cesium.

We calculated the limit of detection (LOD) from the obtained result, which was found to be 24 nM. The LOD was calculated using the formula LOD = 3S/q, Where S is the standard deviation, and q is the slope of the calibration curve (Fig. 6b). A comparison of different detecting systems for Cesium determination is summarized in Table 1 .

The practical application of MCQDs was examined by sensing the Cesium in the seawater sample. The different concentration of prepared Cesium was spiked into seawater, tap water and they were subjected to PL measurements and observed a gradual quenching up to 30% of MCQDs intensity. With the linear regression calculation, the limit of detection was calculated to be 40 nM and 37 nM in seawater and tap water respectively. These results confirm that the MCQDs can be used as an effective probe to sense Cesium in real samples (Fig. 7 a–d). The recoveries of the real sample detection were presented in Table S1.

Interferents such as arsenic, lead, cadmium, nickel, mercury, zinc, chromium was used for the interferents study. The experiment is proceeded as follows where 100 μM interferent solutions were prepared. In cuvette 100 μL MCQDs were taken, to which the prepared interferent solution were added and 3 mL of DI were added to cuvette and allowed to incubate for 5 min. Then the PL spectra were recorded and analyzed. The PL spectra results shows that only cesium quenched the intensity of MCQDs (Fig. S4), where there is no decrease in intensity of fluorescence with interferents other than cesium. This shows that the MCQDs were selective towards cesium.

From the CV studies of (Fig. 8 a), a comparative illustration of the redox probe in both acidic and basic medium. Alkaline medium shows promising redox activities. The ECSA value estimated to be around 9.48, 10.06, 14.24, 36.89 and 40.48 cm2/g attained for M-CQDs in various electrolytic mediums (H2SO4, KOH, Glycerol, H2SO4+Glycerol and KOH + Glycerol). Similarly, the CV study had been performed for bare GCE with respect to various solvents (Fig. S5a), and we did not observe any peaks as observed for M-CQDs modified GCE which confirms that our material is highly sensitive for the glycerol oxidation. The ECSA study exposes that the variation in the electrolytic medium plays a crucial role in the glycerol oxidation process. At the same time, the maximum ECSA attained using KOH + Glycerol is 4 times higher than that of individual KOH solvent.

The linear sweep voltammogram studies for bare GCE with respect to various solvents and it could be observed a straight line since there is no interaction between the electrode surface and solvents are depicted from Fig. S5 b). Fig. 8b shows the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves corresponding to the geometrical current densities (mA/cm2) of the catalyst modified electrode in various solvents in the presence and absence of Glycerol. In the absence of Glycerol, the M-CQDs catalyst propels the anodic OER similar to the overall water splitting process in a potential range of 1.38 V vs RHE (KOH) and 1.49 V vs RHE (H2SO4) at a constant current density of 10 mA/cm2. Upon blending glycerol probe for the catalytic response of M-CQDs catalyst, the anodic peaks shift significantly to 1.32 V vs RHE (KOH) and 1.46 V vs RHE (H2SO4), insinuating that electrokinetics of glycerol oxidation is convincingly beneficial than water catalysis. Interestingly, the change in catalytic onset potential attributes to the more convincing glycerol oxidation to high-value products. The glycerol oxidation performances of the M-CQDs catalyst derive anodic responses from the catalysts themselves because the bare GCE is catalytically inactive toward glycerol oxidation. While examining the catalytic oxidation of glycerol with M-CQDs, the production of formic acid is highly selective compared with other value-added products because of the C–C cleavage of the synthesized material. Upon the core –OH oxidation of glycerol, produces glyceraldehyde (GLAD) (one-step oxidation) is attained, whereas the only oxidation of ancillary –OH produces dihydroxyacetone (DHA) (Liu et al., 2018, 2020). GLAD and DHA oxidation of residual –OH leads to the formation of C2 products, glyceric acid is further oxidized to lactic acid, acetic acid, formic acid after experiencing with the cleavage of one C–C bond during the oxidation processes (Alaba et al., 2020). From the previous reports, it could be concluded that at an oxidation potential of 1.3 V (vs. RHE) produces formic acid with maximum efficiency of 80.6% [Han et al., 2020] (Table S3).

The Nyquist plot analysis illustrated from Fig. 8c, at the potential of 0.19 V, unravelling the resistance of the electrocatalysts. The results reveal that variation in electrolyte medium declines the x-intercept of M-CQDs composite, thereby lowering the bulk resistance (Rs) and the contact resistance (Rct) at the interface between the current collector and the electrocatalyst [Gao et al., 2018]. The quasi-semicircle is an essential parameter for determining the electrical conductivity of the fabricated composite electrocatalysts. Comparative table of charge transfer resistance from EIS spectrum response were presented in Table S2. This result shows that M-CQDs in KOH + Glycerol exhibits the slightest electron transfer resistance and the best electrical conductivity. The improved activity attributed to the defect created on the interfacial surface enhanced the hydrophilicity of its surface with an increase in the hydrophilic functional groups, thereby promoting the mass transfer between the electrolyte and the catalytic composites [Le et al., 2017]. The anticipated products of glycerol oxidation using M-CQDs include glycerate, dihydroxyacetone, tartronate, mesoxalate, which could effectively minimize the costs of hydrogen generation [Weber et al., 2018].

The chronoamperometric studies of the electrodes in 0.5 M KOH + Glycerol is displayed in Fig. 8d, whose measurement exposes the electrochemical activity and stability of the M-CQDs electrode. The glycerol conversion occurred rapidly in an acidic and alkaline medium and reached a stable state until 8 h. These results show that M-CQDs is the best electrocatalyst and alkaline media is the most suitable for glycerol oxidation.

As a result of this, we have synthesized carbon quantum dots using maple tree leaves as carbon sources by one-step hydrothermal technique. M-CQDs are an excellent alternative for radioactive element sensing and electrocatalytic glycerol oxidation to formic acid, thereby holding meritorious features like dispersibility, biocompatibility, and reliably conductive. The synthesized M-CQDs are subjected to PL study and found to have a blue emission spectrum at 445 nm upon excitation at 360 nm. In FTIR, Raman and XRD spectroscopy analysis, the standard exclusive patterns of CQDs is observed, suggesting that synthesized M-CQDs are highly amorphous. M-CQDs can be utilized as a novel fluorescence probe to sense radioactive isotope Cesium with the detection limit of 24 nM in the linear range of 100 nM −100 μM, which would be beneficial for precise detection of Cesium in environmental water samples like seawater holding a detection limit of 40 nM. Upon electrocatalytic oxidation of glycerol, the carbon ions feature the intrinsic ability by activating the interfacial layer on the electrode surface. This work paves a new path for exploring radioisotope detection (Cesium) in environmental samples and catalytic oxidation of glycerol to generate formic acid and other value-added products.

Gayathri Chellasamy: Formal analysis; Investigation; Data curation; Writing – original draft. Shiva Kumar Arumugasamy: Formal analysis; Investigation; Validation; Writing – original draft. Saravanan Govindaraju: Resources; Visualization; Writing-review & editing; Supervision; Project administration. Kyusik Yun: Resources; Supervision; Project administration; Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation (2021R1A2C1011858). This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea(NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (2021R1A6A1A03038996).
